title: The Macro Minute: Is this the start of WWIII?

Happy Tuesday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dale,
presenting our macro minute for Tuesday,
March 3rd, 2026. As always, we'll start
with the executive summary from today's
lead off morning note. So, let's dive
right in.
Today's key macro question is, is this
the start of World War III?
Our answer? Highly unlikely.
The highest probability outcome is that
this, too, shall pass. And once it does,
it will leave asset markets in a much
healthier condition with respect to the
crowded bullish positioning we've been
warning about since the start of
November.
Here are the most important things we
learned today regarding the six key
macro cycles that influence the momentum
and dispersion within and across asset
markets on a trend basis, starting with
the growth cycle. The February ISM
manufacturing PMI data did not support
our outlook for accelerating growth or
slowing inflation, and generally did not
support our bullish paradigm CT. The
sequential cooling of the ISM
manufacturing new orders inventory
spread suggests momentum in the US
industrial cycle is not yet on solid
footing.
Shifting to inflation, an advisor to
Iran's
Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps
commander warned, "Forces will set fire
to any ship attempting to pass through
the Strait of Hormuz, amplifying
systemic risk to regional transit
corridors."
China, the primary purchaser of Iran's
oil exports, called on all sides to
guarantee secure maritime transit
through the Strait of Hormuz, where
shipping flows have effectively ceased.
Iraq has begun curtailing production in
the Rumaila oil field operated by BP as
storage capacity becomes constrained
amid tanker congestion in the Persian
Gulf.
These measures will result in a loss 3
million barrels per day from global
supply as long as wartime conditions
endure.
Elsewhere, the February ISM
manufacturing PMI data strongly
supported our sticky inflation theme.
Additionally, the preliminary February
CPIs from the Eurozone supported our
outlook for stable inflation in the
Eurozone economy. Inflation in the Euro
area is trending nicely at or slightly
above 2%. Shifting to monetary policy,
the Q4 corporate sales, profits, and
capex data generally supported our
outlook for accelerating growth in
Japan. The January unemployment rate
data did not, however, and may give the
BOJ scope to delay further monetary
policy normalization.
Further delays may exacerbate the
sell-off in long-duration JGBs amid
fiscal reflation by the politically
emboldened Takaichi government. Uh
nothing actionable with respect to
fiscal policy or liquidity. And finally,
with respect to positioning, uh global
risk assets repriced lower alongside
global sovereign debt as the US's
congressionally unauthorized war with
Iran continued to escalate, catalyzing
intensifying market anxiety around a
protracted energy supply shock and an
attendant acceleration in inflation.
US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth uh on
Monday dismissed the premise of a quote
endless war with Iran. Yet President
Trump subsequently stated operations
could extend quote far longer than an
initial four-to-five-week estimate and
that the US would do quote whatever it
takes.
Both leaders declined to exclude the
deployment of American ground forces.
Recall that on Monday, Secretary of
State Marco Rubio indicated the
objective was to dismantle Iran's
ballistic missile program and naval
capabilities to neutralize threats to
global shipping lanes. Meanwhile,
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin
Netanyahu wrote on social media that the
effort was also intended to create the
conditions for the brave Iranian people
to cast off the yoke of tyranny. Um so,
it sounds like a lot of different
crosscurrents from an objective
standpoint. And more crosscurrents from
an objective standpoint, in our view,
increases the probability that this
lasts for uh you know, longer than any
of us would hope for it to last. Uh but,
as we said, you know, in terms of the
answer to the today's key macro
question, highest probability outcome is
that this too shall pass. And once it
does, it will leave asset will likely
leave asset markets in a much healthier
condition with respect to the crowded
bulls positioning that we've been
warning about and that a lot of
investors have been taking on the chin
in terms of some violent chop and unwind
in some very crowded factors since
October-November. So,
as always, NA equals nothing actionable
with respect to our Bayesian priors.
Continue to review reviewing to learn
whether our institutional grade risk
management overlays, Kissing Doctor Mo,
are evolving their signals in response
to these or anticipated fundamental
dynamics.
Transition to our 42M dashboard.
As always, we wrap up with a question
from our community. This one's titled
Crashy, Crashy. Anybody else worried
about a crash underway?
I'll tell you who's not worried. It's me
and thousands of investors in our global
investor community with the exception of
this this this young
our friend here. Uh
When you have your portfolios being
guided by extremely effective
institutional grade risk management
overlays, which are what Kissing Doctor
Mo are, you don't have to live in fear
about a crash to the downside. You don't
have to live in fear of a melt up to the
upside because, by the way,
spoiler alert, risk works in both
directions and is always a function of
your legacy positions. You're
underweight or short heading into a bull
market, that causes a type two error
that you'll eventually have to
capitulate with FOMO and cause you to
make a very hazardous choice at or near
a market top. If you are
overweight or, you know, levered long
heading into a
crash, then that'll cause you to make a
type two error that you'll eventually
have to capitulate
into FOMO, which is fear of more losses,
you know, at or near a market low.
Markets don't bottom
until people stop selling.
Markets don't top until people stop
buying. And so, if you're, you know,
committing a type two error, which is,
you know, again, you're long when you're
not supposed to be or
short when you're not supposed to be or
underweight when you're not supposed to
be or overweight when you're not
supposed to be, you know, you which
essentially means you're fighting our
Kissing Doctor Mo signals. When you
fight our Kissing Doctor Mo signals, you
expose your portfolios to the the risk
of type two errors, which are more ha
more hazardous kinds of errors from the
perspective of trying to achieve your
strategic investment objectives. If you
divide it a you turn distribution into
four quantiles, type two errors live on
the left side of the left side the left
it's the leftmost part of those
quantiles. The left side of the left
tail of the distribution and that's
where the biggest errors come from. So
you know my my
the key takeaway from this education
today is that you know if you're
systematically and more importantly
dispassionately
executing our systematic institutional
grade risk management overlays KISS for
retail investors Dr. Mo for
institutional investors and/or retail
investors who want to optimize for
taking back the risk as opposed to
retiring on time and comfortably, if
you're systematically and
dispassionately executing those, you
don't have to live in fear of any of
these market outcomes. KISS and Dr. Mo
aren't going to sell the top, aren't
going to buy the bottom, but they will
sell very close to the top and they will
buy very close to the bottom and rinse
and repeat and if you do that for a long
enough period across enough market
cycles, you're guaranteed to retire on
time and comfortably. Uh so we'll wrap
it up there.
Uh key takeaway is no need to worry.
It's part of the game.
This is why you're DIY investor.
If you don't like this part of the game,
give your money to someone else to
manage.
Or or or or just systematically and
dispassionately execute our KISS and Dr.
Mo signals which in my opinion is a
probably better outcome because most
money managers aren't having their
portfolio allocation decisions managed
by KISS and Dr. Mo. Quite a few of them
are though.
I think we're up to
25 30 trillion dollars in aggregate AUM
across our global buy side clients. So
really blessed by that, really blessed
by your viewership and we will let you
go. Everyone have a wonderful day. Catch
